subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — BOG3/EOG Released Form
instructions: Released-form sample. Choose the best answer for each question.

passages.title: The Great Escape
passages.text:
Every summer, the county fair came to town. And in the middle of the poultry tent sat Rhode Island Red, the rooster. He was a fine, proud bird with bright red feathers and a loud crow.

One morning, Red pecked at the latch on his cage. He pecked and pecked until the door swung open. Red hopped out and looked around. Then he headed straight for the goose tent. He used his beak to lift the latch on the goose cage. The geese waddled out, honking happily. Red did the same for the rabbits, the ducks, and even the goats. Soon all the animals were free!

The animals raced through the fairgrounds. They went to the midway, where the rides and games were. The geese rode the bumper cars. The goats ate cotton candy. The ducks splashed in a water game. Red stood on top of a prize booth and watched. He was having the time of his life.

The boy who took care of the animals heard the noise and came running. He saw the empty cages and the animals running wild. He knew he had to get them back. But chasing them would never work.

The boy ran to the feed shed and grabbed a pail of oats. He shook the pail so the oats rattled. The animals loved oats. The boy walked slowly toward the animal tent, dropping a trail of oats behind him. One by one, the animals followed the trail back to their cages. The boy was clever and patient.

But Rhode Island Red would not follow the oats. The boy had to grab him first. Once Red was back in his cage, the boy closed all the latches tightly.

Rhode Island Red just drank a long drink, fluffed up his feathers, and crowed, "R-r-r-r-roooo!" He was very pleased with himself. He had let the animals out of their cages so they could have some fun. And that made him the happiest rooster at the fair.

passages.title: Under My Nose
passages.text:
My name is Tom, and I make books for children. I love to draw and write stories. But getting a good idea for a book is the hardest thing for me. It is also the most fun.

When I was young, I loved to read picture books. My favorite stories were about animals. One day, my teacher read a book about a brave mouse. That story inspired me. I wanted to make my own books someday.

I went to art school to learn how to draw. I practiced drawing people, trees, houses, and animals. My teacher taught me how to paint a portrait. A portrait is a picture of a person or animal that shows what they really look like.

At home, I have a cat named Whiskers. She likes to sit on my desk while I work. I put a bell on her collar. The bell warns the birds when she goes outside. The birds hear the bell and fly away before she can sneak up on them.

When I start a new book, I do many things. First, I get an idea. Then I think about the characters. Next, I write out rough story ideas for my books. After that, I make thumbnail sketches. Thumbnail sketches are tiny drawings that show what happens on each page.

Then I read the text out loud and listen to the rhythm of the words. I want the story to sound smooth and musical when someone reads it. If the words sound clunky, I change them.

Sometimes I get stuck. I cannot think of a good idea. But I have learned that ideas are everywhere. They are right under your nose. I just have to look around me. A funny dog at the park, a rainy day, or a slice of pizza can all become a story.

Making books is hard work, but I love it. I hope my books make kids smile.

passages.title: Octopus
passages.text: The octopus is a sea animal. It has an interesting body. First, it has three hearts. The heart is often seen as a symbol of love. That’s a lot of love! An octopus also has eight limbs. Some people call them arms or tentacles. Eight arms could give a lot of hugs, but the octopus is a very shy animal. If an octopus is scared, it will squirt ink and swim away.

passages.title: The Adventures of Grandfather Frog
passages.text:
Billy Mink ran around the edge of the Smiling Pool and turned down by the Laughing Brook. His eyes twinkled with mischief, and he hurried as only Billy can. As he passed Jerry Muskrat's house, Jerry saw him.

"Hi, Billy Mink! Where are you going in such a hurry this fine morning?" he called.

"To find Little Joe Otter. Have you seen him?" replied Billy.

"No," said Jerry. "He's probably down to the Big River fishing. I heard him say last night that he was going."

"Thanks," said Billy Mink, and without waiting to say more he was off like a little brown flash.

Jerry watched him out of sight. "Hump!" exclaimed Jerry. "Billy Mink is in a terrible hurry this morning. Now I wonder what he is so anxious to find Little Joe Otter for. When they get their heads together, it is usually for some mischief."

Jerry climbed to the top of his house and looked over the Smiling Pool in the direction from which Billy Mink had just come. Almost at once he saw Grandfather Frog fast asleep on his big green lily pad. The legs of a foolish green fly were sticking out of one corner of his big mouth. Jerry couldn't help laughing, for Grandfather Frog certainly did look funny.

"He's had a good breakfast this morning, and his full stomach has made him sleepy," thought Jerry. "But he's getting careless in his old age. He certainly is getting careless. The idea of going to sleep right out in plain sight like that!"

Suddenly a new thought popped into his head. "Billy Mink saw him, and that is why he is so anxious to find Little Joe Otter. He is planning to play some trick on Grandfather Frog as sure as pollywogs have tails!" exclaimed Jerry. Then his eyes began to twinkle as he added: "I think I'll have some fun myself."

Without another word Jerry slipped down into the water and swam over to the big green lily pad of Grandfather Frog. Then he pounded the water loudly with his tail. Grandfather Frog’s big goggly eyes flew open, and he was just about to make a frightened plunge into the Smiling Pool when he saw Jerry.

“Have a nice nap?” inquired Jerry, with a broad grin.

“I wasn’t asleep!” protested Grandfather Frog indignantly. “I was just thinking.”

“Don’t you think it a rather dangerous plan to think so long with your eyes closed?” asked Jerry.

“Well, maybe I did just doze off,” admitted Grandfather Frog sheepishly.

“Maybe you did,” replied Jerry. “Now listen.” Then Jerry whispered in Grandfather Frog’s ear, and both chuckled as if they were enjoying some joke, for they are great friends, you know. Afterward Jerry swam back to his house, and Grandfather Frog closed his eyes so as to look just as he did when he was asleep.

Meanwhile Billy Mink had hurried down the Laughing Brook. Halfway to the Big River he met Little Joe Otter bringing home a big fish, for you know Little Joe is a great fisherman. Billy Mink hastened to tell him how Grandfather Frog had fallen fast asleep on his big green lily pad.

“It’s a splendid chance to have some fun with Grandfather Frog and give him a great scare,” concluded Billy.

Little Joe Otter put his fish down and grinned. He likes to play pranks almost as well as he likes to go fishing.

“What can we do?” said he.

passages.title: Beavers at Home
passages.text:
A beaver is a wild animal about three feet in length, and weighing forty or fifty pounds. It is covered with fine, glossy, grayish brown fur. Its tail is nearly a foot long, and has no hair at all, but only little scales, something like those of a fish. When the beaver is swimming about in the water it uses its tail as a kind of rudder.

A beaver cannot bear to live alone. He is never as happy as when he has a large number of friends close at hand whom he can visit every day; for beavers are the best and kindest neighbors in the world, always ready to help one another in building new houses or in repairing old ones.

Of course the first thing to be done when one is going to build a house or a village is to find a good place for it; and the spot which every beaver of sense thinks is best is either a large pond, or, if no pond is to be had, a low plain with a stream running through it. For on such a plain, a pond can be made by causing the water to cover it.

It must be a very, very long time since beavers first learned that the way to make a pond is to build a dam across a running stream. To begin with, they must know which way the stream runs, and in this they never make a mistake.

They first gather together a number of sticks and logs about five feet long, which they carry or roll into the stream. While some of the beavers are doing this—for the safety of the village lies in the strength of the foundation—others are gathering and piling up many green branches of trees. These branches, which they have cut from the trees with their teeth, are piled among the sticks and logs, and soon a dam is formed that reaches across the stream.

When the foundation of the dam has been finished, the beavers pile stones and mud upon it until they have built a wall ten or twelve feet thick at the bottom and two or three feet thick at the top. After all this has been done, the older and wiser beavers go carefully over every part to see if the dam is of the right shape and is strong and safe; for beavers do not like poor work, and they know that a weak dam is easily washed away.

When the dam has been finished and the pond made, the beavers begin to think about their houses. As they like to have their rooms dry, they raise the floors of their houses some distance above the water, so that when the stream rises during the rainy season they will not be flooded.

Beavers are always quite clear in their minds as to what they want, and how to get it, and they like to keep things separate. When they are in the water, they are as happy as they can be; but when they are out of it, they like to be dry. It is sometimes two or three months before the village is finished. But the little round huts are to be used only for winter homes; for during the summer no beaver would think of sleeping indoors or, indeed, of staying very long in the same place.

Everything that a beaver does is well done. The walls of his house are thick and strong, and when he has a large family or many friends to stay with him the house has several rooms in it. No beaver ever thinks of living alone. Sometimes he will have one companion, and sometimes a dozen or more. But however full the house may be, everything is kept in good order. Each beaver has his fixed place on the floor, which is covered with dry leaves and grass. A door is always kept open into the place where their food is kept, and so they never go hungry. There they stay all through the winter eating the bark and tender shoots of young trees which they have carefully stored away, sleeping through the cold stormy weather, and at last getting very fat.

At one time there were many beavers in the West and the South, but now there are very few to be found there. Many years ago a Frenchman who was traveling in Louisiana spent a good deal of time watching beavers and learning about their ways. He hid himself close to a dam which the little creatures had built, and in the night he cut a hole about a foot wide right through it.

He had made no noise while cutting through the dam, but the rush of the water awakened one beaver who was not sleeping as soundly as the others. This beaver left his hut quickly, and swam to the dam to see what was wrong. As soon as he saw the stream that had been dug, he struck four loud blows with his tail, and every beaver in the village left his bed and rushed out in answer to the call. When they reached the dam and saw the large hole in it, they held a meeting as to what they should do. Then the head beaver gave orders to the rest, and all went to the bank to get sticks and mud.

When they had gathered together as much as they could carry, they formed in line and marched with their loads to the dam. The sticks were thrown into the hole and mud and stones were packed upon them. The beavers worked hard and wisely, and in a short time the dam was as good as ever. Then one of the older beavers struck two blows with his tail, and in a few minutes all were in bed and asleep again.

passages.title: Dog a Hero on Mount Hood
passages.text:
Mount Hood is a tall mountain in Oregon. Many people love to climb it. On a bright Saturday morning, a group of three teachers went rock climbing on Mount Hood. They were experienced climbers. They had climbed many mountains before.

They brought their dog, Velvet, with them. Velvet was a strong German shepherd with thick black and brown fur. She loved to hike and explore.

The climbers hiked up the mountain. They climbed over rocks and ice. Velvet ran ahead, sniffing the ground. The weather was sunny and clear. Everything seemed perfect.

On Sunday, the weather took a turn for the worse. Dark clouds rolled in. The wind blew hard. Snow began to fall. The climbers knew it was time to go down. They ended their climb early because the weather was bad.

They hiked for miles to try to make it down the mountainside. But the mountain was slippery. Ice covered the rocks. One of the climbers slipped and fell. Then another slipped. They tumbled down the steep slope.

Velvet barked loudly. She ran to the climbers. They were hurt and cold. The temperature was freezing. The climbers knew they were in big trouble. They had to face the fact that they might not make it down the mountain alive.

Velvet stayed close to them. She lay down next to the climbers. They petted her thick fur. Velvet licked their faces. Both the petting and the licking calmed the climbers. They felt less scared.

Velvet kept them warm with her body heat. She never left their side. Hours passed. Finally, a rescue team heard Velvet's barks. The rescue team found the climbers with Velvet in White River Canyon at about 7,400 feet. The climbers were saved!

They had no idea a German shepherd named Velvet would help save their lives. Velvet was a true hero.

questions.questionText: What does shy mean in the text?
options: A fearful
B harmful
C proud
D brave
questions.explanation: Shy means feeling scared or nervous around others, so it makes sense that it relates to being fearful.

questions.questionText: How many hearts does an octopus have?
options: A one
B three
C five
D eight
questions.explanation: An octopus has three hearts, which helps it pump blood, so saying it has five is incorrect because that's too many!

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of pecked in paragraph 2?
options: A poked
B ran from
C marched to
D tossed
questions.explanation: Pecked means to poke at something, like a bird pecking at food, so tossing is not the right meaning here.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of latch in paragraph 2?
options: A side
B fence
C lock
D bottom
questions.explanation: A latch is a type of lock that keeps things closed, just like how a door lock keeps a door shut.

questions.questionText: What did Rhode Island Red do after he got out of his cage?
options: A He rode with the animals on the roller coaster.
B He put all of the animals in their cages.
C He chased the animals to the midway.
D He let the other animals out of their cages.
questions.explanation: Rhode Island Red had fun riding the roller coaster with the other animals, which shows he was enjoying himself after getting out.

questions.questionText: How did the boy get all the animals back in their cages?
options: A He chased after each one to get them to run into their cages.
B He shook a pail of oats while yelling at the animals to get back in their cages.
C He dropped a trail of oats behind him so the animals would follow him to their cages.
D He grabbed Rhode Island Red first and then scared all the other animals to their cages.
questions.explanation: He grabbed Rhode Island Red first because that helped him scare the other animals back into their cages, showing he was clever in his plan.

questions.questionText: Why is Rhode Island Red so pleased with himself at the end of the text?
options: A He gets the boy to feed all the animals and let them out of their cages.
B He gets all the animals back into their cages without anyone else’s help.
C He is happy to be in his cage with a long drink and some food.
D He is happy that he let the animals out of their cages so they could have some fun.
questions.explanation: Rhode Island Red felt proud because he let the other animals have fun, and that made him happy to see them enjoying themselves.

questions.questionText: Which word describes the boy in the text?
options: A clever
B scared
C unusual
D useless
questions.explanation: The boy is clever because he comes up with smart ideas to lead and manage the animals, showing he thinks well on his feet.

questions.questionText: What is one way that Rhode Island Red and the boy are alike?
options: A They both lead the animals.
B They both chase the animals.
C They both feed the animals.
D They both catch the animals.
questions.explanation: Rhode Island Red and the boy are alike because they both know how to lead the animals, which shows teamwork.

questions.questionText: Which statement from the text shows that Rhode Island Red is proud?
options: A “And in the middle of the poultry tent sat Rhode Island Red, the rooster.”
B “One morning, Red pecked at the latch on his cage.”
C “Rhode Island Red headed straight for the goose tent.”
D “But Rhode Island Red just drank a long drink, fluffed up his feathers, and crowed, ‘R-r-r-r-roooo!’ ”
questions.explanation: Rhode Island Red is proud when he fluffs up his feathers and crows, showing he feels good about himself and what he did.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of inspired in paragraph 4?
options: A changed
B started
C finished
D used
questions.explanation: The word 'inspired' means to start something new, like when you get a great idea and want to create something special.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what is often difficult for the author?
options: A drawing pictures
B creating a title
C getting started
D reading a book
questions.explanation: The author finds it hard to get started because beginning a new project can be tricky, just like starting a puzzle can be tough until you find the first piece.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of portrait in paragraph 6?
options: A lesson
B picture
C report
D story
questions.explanation: A 'portrait' is a picture of someone, usually showing their face, which helps us remember what they look like.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why does the cat have a bell on its collar?
options: A The bell warns the birds.
B The bell makes a nice sound.
C The bell is pretty.
D The bell is interesting.
questions.explanation: The bell on the cat's collar is there to warn the birds, so they can fly away before the cat gets too close.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the writing steps in paragraphs 10 and 11?
options: A by contrasting two different styles for writing books
B by giving a step-by-step process for publishing a book
C by comparing the writing steps for two kinds of books
D by giving a chronological order for beginning a book
questions.explanation: The author connects the writing steps by showing the order in which you should do things when starting to write a book, like following a recipe.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in paragraphs 12 and 13?
options: A by listing important events that have occurred in the author’s life
B by explaining the different parts of the book that have to be made
C by describing how the author gets ideas when they just will not come
D by comparing the rhythm of the text when read aloud to how it looks on paper
questions.explanation: The author connects the ideas by explaining the different parts of a book that need to be created, which helps us understand how a book is made.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the phrase “right under your nose” in paragraph 16?
options: A inside a person
B on a person’s face
C read by a person
D in front of a person
questions.explanation: The phrase 'right under your nose' means something is in front of you, like when you can't find your glasses because they're on your head!

questions.questionText: Which statement by the author supports the main idea?
options: A "The books I made needed text, so I began writing words to go with my art."
B "Getting a good idea for a book is the hardest thing for me, but also the most fun."
C "I like to write out rough story ideas for my books, then make thumbnail sketches."
D "I like to read the text out loud and listen to the rhythm of the words."
questions.explanation: This statement shows that getting a good idea is really important and can be hard, which is a big part of writing a book and supports the main idea.

questions.questionText: Why was Billy in a hurry?
options: A He was looking for Grandfather Frog.
B He was getting careless in his old age.
C He was going to eat breakfast.
D He was looking for Little Joe Otter.
questions.explanation: Billy was in a hurry because he was looking for Little Joe Otter, which means he wanted to find his friend quickly!

questions.questionText: Why was Grandfather Frog sleeping?
options: A He was hot.
B He is old.
C He had just eaten.
D He had been sick.
questions.explanation: Grandfather Frog was sleeping because he had just eaten, and sometimes after a big meal, we feel sleepy too.

questions.questionText: Why does Jerry laugh when he sees Grandfather Frog?
options: A Grandfather Frog is jumping on the lily pads.
B Grandfather Frog has part of a fly sticking out of his mouth.
C Grandfather Frog has singing lessons after breakfast with his friends.
D Grandfather Frog is dancing because he is happy to see Jerry.
questions.explanation: Jerry laughs because he sees Grandfather Frog with part of a fly in his mouth, which is funny and surprising!

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word slipped in paragraph 10?
options: A crashed
B floated
C attacked
D entered
questions.explanation: In this paragraph, 'slipped' means to enter, like when someone quietly goes into a room without being noticed.

questions.questionText: Why did Grandfather Frog close his eyes to look just as he did when he was asleep?
options: A to play a joke on Billy
B to get back to his nap
C to be a friend to Jerry
D to finish eating the fly
questions.explanation: Grandfather Frog closes his eyes to play a joke on Billy, making it seem like he is asleep when he really isn't!

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of splendid in paragraph 17?
options: A fast
B great
C safe
D usual
questions.explanation: The word 'splendid' means great, which shows something is really wonderful or impressive!

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text supports that Grandfather Frog was scared when Jerry pounded the water with his tail?
options: A “The idea of going to sleep right out in plain sight like that!”
B “He was just about to make a frightened plunge into the Smiling Pool when he saw Jerry.”
C “I wasn’t asleep!’ protested Grandfather Frog indignantly.”
D “Don’t you think it a rather dangerous plan to think so long with your eyes closed?’ asked Jerry.”
questions.explanation: This detail shows that Grandfather Frog was scared because he was about to jump into the water quickly when he saw Jerry, which means he was startled!

questions.questionText: How does Jerry’s climb to the top of his house contribute to the rest of the text?
options: A He realizes that he wants to take a nap.
B He knows that he wants to go fishing.
C He knows where to find Longlegs the Blue Heron.
D He realizes why Billy Mink is in such a hurry.
questions.explanation: Jerry's climb helps him understand why Billy Mink is in a hurry, showing how climbing gives him a better view of what's happening!

questions.questionText: According to the text, which choice describes a beaver’s tail?
options: A It is three feet long and weighs forty or fifty pounds.
B It has small scales similar to those of a fish.
C It is covered with shiny, brown fur.
D It looks like a hairy rudder on a boat.
questions.explanation: A beaver's tail has small scales like a fish, which makes it different from other animal tails and helps it in the water!

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word bear in paragraph 2?
options: A accept
B carry
C uncover
D earn
questions.explanation: In this paragraph, 'bear' means to accept something, like when you take on a responsibility or deal with a situation.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what step follows finishing the foundation when building a beaver dam?
options: gathering sticks and logs
piling stones and mud on top
watching which way the stream runs
checking to see if it is strong and safe
questions.explanation: After finishing the foundation of a beaver dam, the next step is piling stones and mud on top to make it strong and sturdy!

questions.questionText: What is the connection between paragraphs 5 and 6?
options: They present a problem and offer a solution.
They explain a cause-effect relationship.
They present a sequence for building.
They compare habits of wise beavers.
questions.explanation: Paragraphs 5 and 6 show a sequence for building, explaining the steps that come one after the other in the process of making a dam.

questions.questionText: Why do beavers carefully inspect a dam after building it?
options: Beavers need to build a bridge to get across the stream.
Beavers know that water will wash away a weak dam.
Beavers want to find the best place to build a house or village.
Beavers make the walls of their houses with big sticks.
questions.explanation: Beavers check their dam because they want to make sure it's strong enough to hold the water. If it's weak, the water can wash it away, so they inspect it carefully to keep their home safe.

questions.questionText: What does the word companion mean in paragraph 9?
options: sister
enemy
worker
friend
questions.explanation: The word companion means friend because it describes someone who is close to you and shares fun times, just like a good friend does.

questions.questionText: How are paragraphs 10 and 11 connected?
options: Both compare the two ways that beavers build dams.
Both describe the differences between a dam and a pond.
Paragraph 10 presents a problem, and paragraph 11 describes a solution.
Paragraph 10 shows a result of the unusual situation in paragraph 11.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 10 talks about a problem that the beavers face, and then paragraph 11 gives a solution to that problem, showing how they work together to fix things.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text supports the main idea?
options: “A beaver is a wild animal about three feet in length.”
“They first gather together a number of sticks and logs.”
“No beaver ever thinks of living alone.”
“Each beaver has his fixed place on the floor.”
questions.explanation: The detail about gathering sticks and logs shows the main idea of how beavers build their homes, which is important to understand their behavior.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of experienced in paragraph 2?
options: eager
important
practiced
expected
questions.explanation: Experienced means practiced because it describes someone who has done something many times and knows how to do it well.

questions.questionText: Why did the rock climbers end their climb early?
options: The weather was bad.
The group was bored.
The dog was tired.
The dog was sick.
questions.explanation: The climbers ended their climb early because the weather was bad, which can make climbing unsafe and uncomfortable.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what caused the climbers to fall down the mountain?
options: They were very tired.
The mountain was rocky.
It was getting dark.
The mountain was slippery.
questions.explanation: The climbers fell because the mountain was slippery, which means it was hard to keep their balance and stay safe while climbing.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the point that the climb was very dangerous in paragraphs 5 and 6?
options: A The information in paragraph 6 is a result of the action in paragraph 5.
B The ideas in paragraphs 5 and 6 compare the different climbers.
C Paragraphs 5 and 6 describe the path the climbers took.
D Paragraph 5 lists the steps needed for paragraph 6.
questions.explanation: The author connects the danger of the climb by showing that what happens in paragraph 6 is a result of the actions described in paragraph 5, making it clear how risky the situation was.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of “face the fact” in paragraph 8?
options: A to look at an idea one believes in
B to admit that something is true
C to escape from danger
D to learn something new
questions.explanation: To 'face the fact' means to admit that something is true, even if it is scary or hard to believe.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what is the relationship between the climbers petting Velvet’s fur and Velvet licking them?
options: A Both dried the climbers.
B Both scared the climbers.
C Both calmed the climbers.
D Both warmed the climbers.
questions.explanation: Petting Velvet's fur and Velvet licking the climbers both helped calm them down, making them feel safer and less worried.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the text?
options: A Mount Hood can be a dangerous place.
B Mount Hood is popular for rock climbing.
C A dog helps a group of rock climbers.
D A group of teachers goes rock climbing.
questions.explanation: The main idea is about how a dog named Velvet helped the rock climbers. This is important because it shows how the dog played a big role in their adventure and safety.

questions.questionText: Which statement from the text supports the main idea?
options: A “They had no idea a German shepherd named Velvet would help save their lives.”
B “On Sunday, the weather took a turn for the worse.”
C “They hiked for miles to try to make it down the mountainside.”
D “The rescue team found the climbers with Velvet in White River Canyon at about 7,400 feet.”
questions.explanation: This statement tells us that the climbers were surprised that a dog could be so helpful in a dangerous situation. It directly connects to the main idea of the dog helping them.
